coredata.dc:title: Insight into the multiple roles of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots in an ultrathin 2D-0D-2D all-solid-state Z scheme heterostructure and its performance in tetracycline degradation under LED illumination
coredata.dc:creator: Ghosh, Utpal
Pal, Anjali
coredata.dc:description: The rational design of an all-solid-state Z scheme composite through a single-step hydrothermal treatment by combining ultrathin defect engineered graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (DCN), tungsten oxide hydrate nanoplates (W), and nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) has been reported. For the first time, this study demonstrates that NCQD could successfully exfoliate g-C3N4 to produce few-layered thick g-C3N4 nanosheets, besides playing the role of an electron mediator in the composite. The 10%W/1%NCQD/DCN composite exhibited the highest photocatalytic tetracycline degradation efficiency under an 18 W LED illumination. It illustrated a 31.5, 7.7, and 5.2-times higher apparent degradation rate constant (0.044 min−1) than W, bulk g-C3N4, and DCN, respectively. Further, the composite exhibited an outstanding efficacy toward the degradation of various real water matrices. The composite demonstrated high stability and reusability. Furthermore, a plausible all-solid-state Z scheme charge carrier mechanism is proposed based on the results of the radical scavenging experiments.

head.ce:title: Insight into the multiple roles of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots in an ultrathin 2D-0D-2D all-solid-state Z scheme heterostructure and its performance in tetracycline degradation under LED illumination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur 721302, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Civil Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Civil Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur 721302, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rational design of an all-solid-state Z scheme composite through a single-step hydrothermal treatment by combining ultrathin defect engineered graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (DCN), tungsten oxide hydrate nanoplates (W), and nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) has been reported. For the first time, this study demonstrates that NCQD could successfully exfoliate g-C3N4 to produce few-layered thick g-C3N4 nanosheets, besides playing the role of an electron mediator in the composite. The 10%W/1%NCQD/DCN composite exhibited the highest photocatalytic tetracycline degradation efficiency under an 18 W LED illumination. It illustrated a 31.5, 7.7, and 5.2-times higher apparent degradation rate constant (0.044 min−1) than W, bulk g-C3N4, and DCN, respectively. Further, the composite exhibited an outstanding efficacy toward the degradation of various real water matrices. The composite demonstrated high stability and reusability. Furthermore, a plausible all-solid-state Z scheme charge carrier mechanism is proposed based on the results of the radical scavenging experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: Historically, tetracycline (TC), a well-known antibiotic, has been prescribed for treating a wide range of diseases, such as hydatoncus, mycoplasmal pneumonia, etc. [1]. However, the incomplete metabolism of the antibiotics results in the discharge of a high percentage of the consumed antibiotics in the pristine or metabolized form [2,3]. As a result, its abundant presence in various aquatic water matrices has been detected frequently [4]. Its presence poses a severe threat to the ecosystem as it may cause antibiotic resistance, food chain enrichment, and chronic toxic effects [5]. Moreover, TC’s stable chemical structure and recalcitrant nature make the conventional wastewater treatment techniques ineffective [4,6,7]. Among the advanced oxidation methods, photocatalytic degradation has emerged as the most appropriate option considering the cost-effectiveness, negligible secondary pollutant generation, and short duration [3,8].

In the recent past, metal-free g-C3N4 has acquired commendable attention for its potential as a visible-light-driven (VLD) photocatalyst because of its interesting properties such as layered structure, easy and cost-effective synthesis procedure, moderate bandgap (∼2.7 eV), excellent physical and chemical stability, and tunable electronic properties [9–12]. However, the bulk g-C3N4 suffers from limited photocatalytic activity as a result of the quick charge carrier recombination and low surface area [13,14]. Therefore, modification techniques such as exfoliation, defect induction, and heterostructure fabrication have been adopted to mitigate the shortcomings of the pristine material [15–18]. In our previous work, we reported the fabrication of a 3D macroporous structure composed of nitrogen vacant g-C3N4 nanosheets through a solvent-assisted synthesis procedure [19]. However, the efficiency of the as-synthesized catalyst is far from the benchmark for industrial application. Hence, further modifications must be devised to promote the performance of the nitrogen vacant nanosheets.

Fabrication of a Z scheme composite by integrating two semiconductors of matching bandgaps hold great potential in enhancing photocatalytic activity through spatial separation of the charge carriers [20,21]. Fabrication of an all-solid-state Z scheme heterostructure by introducing a solid state electron mediator could further improve the efficiency of Z scheme structures, as they could efficiently curtail the undesired backward reaction, shorten the charge transfer distance and accelerate the electron-hole transfer process [22,23]. However, the high cost of noble metals (Au, Ag, etc.) hampers their application as an electron mediator [24,25]. Therefore, the realization of an alternate electron mediator is of utmost importance.

Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQD), a novel zero-dimensional nanomaterial with a size between 2 and 10 nm, have attracted colossal consideration in photocatalytic application owing to their intriguing optical and electrical properties [26,27]. The presence of conjugated π structures not only imparts the excellent electron reservoir and transfer but also helps in the hybridization with other semiconductors [22]. It is anticipated that introduction of NCQD as an electron mediator would accelerate charge migration between semiconductors and enhance the spatial charge carrier separation. Besides, NCQDs have been employed for liquid-phase exfoliation of graphite into graphene [28,29]. It is believed that NCQDs assisted exfoliation of g-C3N4 holds great potential in catalysis research.

Recently, ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) nanostructures (nanosheet, nanoplate, etc.) have attained tremendous consideration in VLD catalysis research owing to the multiple advantages over the bulk material [30]. In addition, the 2D nanostructures with high surface area could be an excellent choice as a building block for heterostructures [31,32]. Most importantly, the fabrication of a 2D layered composite by combining ultrathin nanostructures is by far the most effective method to enhance photocatalytic activity [30]. The high surface area helps in better hybridization between layers which in turn boosts the charge carrier separation [33]. Tungsten oxide hydrates (WO3·nH2O; n = 1 and 2), with a typical 2D structure, is getting more and more attention in present times because of its fascinating characteristics such as excellent physical and chemical properties, polytypic structure, and widespread application in the field of gas sensing, electrochromic device, photo-electrochromic device, energy storage, electrocatalysis and photocatalysis [15,34–41]. Even though WO3·nH2O exhibits superior light-harvesting capacity than WO3 [41], the potential of WO3·nH2O as a VLD photocatalyst has not been investigated rigorously.

Herein, we report the fabrication of a 2D/0D/2D all-solid-state Z scheme heterostructure by integrating WO3·nH2O nanoplates, ultrathin nitrogen vacant g-C3N4, and NCQDs. For the first time, this study validates that NCQDs could act as an electron mediator, and could exfoliate g-C3N4 at the same time to produce ultrathin nanosheets. The as-synthesized catalyst demonstrated excellent efficiency toward TC degradation in various real water matrices (pond water and real wastewater) under LED illumination (18 W). We believe that this study would inspire the fabrication of NCQD based composites and investigate the multiple roles played by NCQDs.

Sigma Aldrich (USA) provided melamine. Nitric acid (70%), ethylene glycol, benzoquinone (BQ), sodium tungstate dihydrate, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), urea, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), and citric acid was procured from Merck (India). HiMedia (India) provided tetracycline. Aldrich chemical company supplied the terephthalic acid. 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N oxide (DMPO) was purchased from TCI chemicals (India). All chemicals used in this study conformed to analytical grade and were consumed as received. Millipore water was used during the study.

The bulk g-C3N4 (marked BCN hereafter) was prepared through the polycondensation reaction of melamine. In detail, 10 g melamine was taken in a covered alumina crucible and heated at 550℃ for 3 h in a muffle furnace. Upon reaching to room temperature, the yellowish residue was collected, ground in a mortar pestle, and stored for future use.

The nitrogen defect engineered g-C3N4 macrostructure (called DCN hereafter) was prepared through the solvent-assisted synthesis procedure reported in our previous work [19]. Specifically, melamine (2 g) was placed in a covered alumina crucible, and 4 mL ethylene glycol was then added to the crucible. After that, the mixture was stirred vigorously with a glass rod to make the solution homogeneous. Thereafter, the crucible was heated at 550℃ for 3 h in a muffle furnace. After cooling to ambient temperature, the whitish-yellow residue was collected, ground, and stored for future use.

Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were synthesized according to a procedure reported elsewhere [42]. In detail, citric acid (3 g) and urea (1 g) were dissolved in deionized water (15 mL) by stirring in a magnetic stirrer. Then, the solution was heated at 180℃ for 5 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. After cooling down, the brown coloured remainder was taken out and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 mins to separate large-sized particles. Subsequently, the solution pH was brought to 7 with 1 N HCl solution followed by heating at 70℃ to get the NCQD powder. Finally, the NCQD powder was dispersed again in deionized water to obtain a stock solution of 10 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of WO3·nH2O/DCN and WO3·nH2O/NCQD/DCN composites
ce:section.ce:para: The WO3·nH2O/DCN heterostructure was synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment. The schematic diagram of the synthesis procedure is detailed in Scheme 1 . Initially, 4.7 mL of 70% HNO3 was slowly added to 25 mL of deionized water (DI) and stirred for 10 min. Then, 250 mg of the as-prepared DCN was added into the solution and ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min. Thereafter, the solution was magnetically stirred for 30 min. An appropriate amount of Na2WO4, 2H2O was dissolved in 5 mL of DI followed by dropwise addition into the DCN solution. Consequently, the solution was further mixed for 30 min before transferring to a Teflon-lined autoclave of 50 mL volume. Consequently, the autoclave was heated at 180℃ for 3 h in a muffle furnace. Upon cooling, the resultant yellowish residue was taken out, washed with DI and ethanol for several times before drying at 60℃ in a hot air oven. Composite with 5, 10, 20, and 30 wt% of WO3·nH2O relative to DCN was fabricated by adding 0.0125 g, 0.025 g, 0.050 g, and 0.075 g Na2WO4, 2H2O, respectively, to the DCN solution. The composites with 5, 10, 20, and 30 wt% of WO3·nH2O were denoted as 5WDCN, 10WDCN, 20WDCN, and 30WDCN, respectively.

The 10% WO3·nH2O/NCQD/DCN composite was fabricated according to the procedure stated above for 10WDCN with an additional step as the incorporation of NCQDs. Specifically, after dispersing DCN for 30 min, an appropriate quantity of NCQD stock solution was poured dropwise to the solution and further stirred for an extra 30 min before the addition of Na2WO4, 2H2O. The rest of the procedure remained the same. 1.25, 2.5, and 3.75 mg of NCQD was added to prepare composites with 0.5, 1, and 1.5% NCQD content, and was marked as 10 W0.5CDCN, 10W1CDCN, and 10 W1.5CDCN, respectively.

The WO3·nH2O nanoplates were synthesized by the same method except for the incorporation of DCN and NCQDs. In detail, 1 g of Na2WO4, 2H2O was added to a solution containing 25 mL DI and 4.7 mL 70% HNO3. Then, the solution was stirred for 30 min in a magnetic stirrer. Afterward, the solution was kept at 180℃ for 3 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. Then, the residue was taken out and washed with DI and ethanol several times, and dried at 60℃ to get the nanoplates. The as-obtained WO3·nH2O nanoplates are denoted as W hereafter.

The XRD spectrum of the samples was acquired in a Malvern Panalytical X-ray diffractometer equipped with Cu kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm). A Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Instruments) was used to record the FTIR spectrum of the samples. XPS analysis was carried out in an ULVAC-PHI5000VersaProbeIII photoelectron spectrophotometer. The morphological features of the samples were observed in a Merlin field emission scanning electron microscope (Carl Zeiss). An FEI Technai G2 20S twin transmission electron microscope was employed to collect the TEM images of the samples. A Cary 5000 spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies) was engaged to record the UV-DRS spectrum of the samples. The surface topography of the samples were revealed by using a 5500 AFM analyzer (Agilent technologies). Photoluminescence measurements were performed in a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse). The BET surface area and pore size distribution of the samples were acquired from a Quantachrome autosorb iQ surface analyzer. The ESR spectra of the superoxide radicals were recorder in a Bruker Elexys ESR spectrophotometer using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N oxide (DMPO) as the spin trapping agent.

The visible light induced activity of the as-prepared composites was assessed by degrading TC under the radiance of an 18 W LED light (Crompton India). The spectrum of the LED is shown in Fig. s1 . The LED light had an intensity of 11.9 ± 0.3 mW/cm2. The LED light intensity was measured with the help of a KM-SPM-530 digital solar power meter (Kasum-Meco, India). The degradation reaction was accomplished in a 50 mL volume closed glass reactor with the LED positioned vertically over the reactor. Initially, a 10 mL aqueous TC solution (TC concentration: 20 mg/L) was added into the reactor. Then, 10 mg of the as-prepared catalyst was placed and ultrasonically dispersed for 5 min before putting in the darkness for 30 min to attain the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Afterwards, the reactor was placed under the LED for a specified time. After that, the solution was extracted, filtered using a 0.22 μm filter paper, and the remaining TC concentration was measured with the help of a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Cary 60, Agilent Technologies).

For the real water samples, the collected samples (pond water and wastewater collected after secondary UASB treatment) were filtered through a 0.22 μm filter paper, and then, the solutions were spiked with 20 mg/L TC concentration. The spiked solution was further used in the degradation process.

The responsible radicals in the degradation process was identified through a series of radical trapping tests by employing 1 mM EDTA-2Na , 1 mM BQ, and 1 mmole IPA as hole (h+), superoxide ( . O2
– ), and hydroxyl ( . OH) scavenger, respectively. Furthermore, the presence of hydroxyl radicals in the reaction system was ascertained from the fluorescence intensity of generated 2-hydroxyterepthalic acid (2 HTA) from the interaction between terephthalic acid and . OH radical. The detailed procedure is as follows: initially, 10 mg 10W1CDCN catalyst was added into a 10 mL aqueous solution comprising 3 mM terephthalic acid and 10 mM NaOH. Thereafter, the solution was placed in the darkness for 30 min. After that, the solution was irradiated with the LED for a stipulated time. Afterward, the solution was collected, filtered using a 0.22 μm filter, and the concentration of 2HTA was measured in a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse) at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm.

The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the samples were measured in a Metrohm Autolab PGSTAT302N electrochemical workstation with a standard three electrode setup equipped with an Ag/AgCl reference electrode and a Pt wire counter electrode. The working electrode was fabricated by coating the photocatalyst on a conductive FTO glass with an effective area of 1 cm2. 0.5 M Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte. The EIS measurements were carried out in the frequency range of 0.5 Hz-105 Hz with a modulating voltage of 10 mV.

The XRD pattern of the DCN, W, 10WDCN, and 10W1CDCN are presented in Fig. 1 a. The sample DCN demonstrated two distinctive peaks at approximately 13° and 27.5°, conforming to the (100) and (002) crystal planes representing in-planar structural motifs and interlayer stacking of conjugated aromatic lattice structures [19,43]. All the sharp diffraction peaks of sample W matched well to the diffraction pattern of WO3·H2O (JCPDS file no. 84–0886). In addition, the two tiny peaks at 23.4° and 24.5° could be attributed to the (200) and (001) crystal plane WO3·2H2O (ICDD file no. 018–1420). The strong and intense peaks of sample W indicate the crystalline nature of the sample. The composites display characteristic peaks of both the precursors, endorsing the successful fabrication of the heterostructure. Moreover, the (002) peak of DCN slightly right-shifted after hydrothermal treatment during composite fabrication, which could have happened from further nitrogen vacancy induction (as confirmed from EPR spectra presented Fig. s2 ) in the skeletal structure of g-C3N4 [44]. Further, the XRD spectrum of 10W1CDCN reveals that NCQD loading did not alter the basic structure of the composite. The strong peak of W at 16.7° and 25.9° corresponding to (020) and (111) plane became weak, whereas the peaks at around 24° got more prominent in the composites. This indicates that the presence of DCN during hydrothermal treatment influences the crystal growing process of W in the composites [45,46].

The surface functionalities of the samples were revealed from the FTIR analysis. As is seen in Fig. 1b, W possesses distinct peaks at around 3390, 1615, 947, and 665 cm−1 which could be ascribed to the presence of O–H, H-O–H, W = O, and O-W-O bonds, respectively [47,48]. The stretching vibration of the N–H bond produced the broad peak of DCN at around 3100 cm−1. The multiple peaks of DCN in the range of 1630–1230 cm−1 appeared due to the skeletal stretching of aromatic carbon and nitrogen heterocycles, whereas the sharp peak at 805 cm−1 corresponds to the out-of-plane vibrational absorption of triazine structural units [49,50]. Both the composite materials exhibited all the characteristic peaks of W as well as DCN. Therefore, it can be said that the heterostructure was successfully synthesized.

The chemical characterization of the catalysts was performed through the XPS analysis. All the spectra of the catalysts were calibrated against the adventitious carbon peak at 284.8 eV. The XPS survey spectra of the 10WDCN and 10W1CDCN are presented in Fig. s3
. As expected, all the elements were observed in the survey spectra of the samples. The deconvolution of the high-resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 1c) of 10WDCN produced two distinctive peaks positioned at 284.8 and 288.1 eV, which is attributed to the adventitious carbon and three coordinated carbons (C-N3), respectively [11]. In the case of 10W1CDCN, the peak at 288.1 is slightly left-shifted as compared to 10WDCN. The high-resolution N1s spectra (Fig. 1d) of the samples are composed of three peaks located at around 398.5, 399.6, and 401.1 eV representing the presence of two coordinated nitrogen (N2c), three coordinated nitrogen (N3c), and terminal amino groups of g-C3N4 skeletal structure [51]. Fig. 1e displays the high-resolution O1s spectra of the samples. The deconvolution of the spectra produced two prominent peaks at 531.2 and 532.7 eV, which corresponds to the characteristic peaks of metal oxides [52]. As presented in Fig. 1f, the deconvolution of W4f high-resolution spectra of 10WDCN produced two distinct peaks at 35.5 and 37.6 eV, which is ascribed to the W4f7/2 and W4f5/2 state of WO3 [53]. A slight left shift in the W4f peak of 10W1CDCN was observed after the incorporation of NCQDs. Overall, a slight negative shift in the binding energy of all the elements were observed in case of 10W1CDCN. This negative shift in BE could have happened due to the movement of the fermi level toward valence band in the composite after NCQD addition [54,55]. Researchers have reported that exfoliation of bulk materials results in downward shift in the work function [56]. Therefore, it could be speculated that this binding energy shift could have resulted from the exfoliation of DCN after the NCQD addition.

The microstructure of the catalysts was revealed through the FESEM and TEM analysis. The SEM and TEM images of the catalysts are detailed in Figs. 2 and 3
. As is seen in Fig. 2a and Fig. 3a, the DCN sample exhibited a self-assembled disorderly shaped porous structure comprised of 2D defect engineered nanosheets. The SAED pattern of DCN (Fig. 3b) confirmed its amorphous nature. On the other hand, sample W possesses a 2D nanoplate morphology of various sizes (Fig. 2b and 3c). The orthorhombic structure of W was ascertained from the SAED pattern in Fig. 3d, which is in accordance with the XRD findings. Further, the sample W demonstrates a lattice spacing of 0.375 nm (Fig. 3g), which corresponds to the (120) plane of WO3·H2O [39]. The SEM image of 10WDCN (Fig. 2c) shows that the hydrothermal synthesis resulted in the successful deposition of the W nanoplates on the 3D macroporous structure of DCN, whereas the addition of CQDs during the synthesis procedure disintegrated the structure (Fig. 2d) to produce an ultrathin nanocomposite. Fig. 3e demonstrates that the 2D W nanoplates were successfully deposited on the exfoliated DCN nanosheets. The SAED pattern of the composite in Fig. 3f further confirmed the strong interaction between the precursors. In addition, the elemental mapping of the 10W1CDCN sample (Fig. 2 (e-h)) confirms the successful heterostructure fabrication. The EDAX pattern of the sample ( Fig. 3j and adjacent table) further validated the existence of all the individual elements in the composite. The 10W1CDCN sample showed an interplanar distance of 0.232 nm representing the (022) plane of W (Fig. 3h) [57]. Thus, it could be said that the heterostructure was successfully fabricated, and NCQDs played a vital role in further exfoliating the DCN structure.

Further, the surface topography of the catalysts were ascertained from the AFM investigation. As observed in Fig. 4 , DCN exhibits an average thickness of 7–8 nm, whereas sample W is composed of nanoplates of an average thickness of 2–3 nm. Surprisingly, the 10W1CDCN sample possesses an average thickness of 4–5 nm. In addition, the 10W0.5CDCN and the 10W1.5CDCN samples also exhibited an average thickness of 5–6 nm ( Fig. s4 ). Therefore, it is evident that the DCN sample was exfoliated to produce ultrathin few-layered DCN after the NCQD addition during hydrothermal treatment.

In addition, the surface area of the samples were obtained from the BET analysis. The result of the BET analysis i.e. pore size distribution and N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm curves are presented in Fig. 5 . As is seen Fig. 5a, typical type IV BET isotherm was followed by all the samples. Moreover, the sample W displayed a H4 hysteresis loop, whereas all the other samples demonstrated a H3 type hysteresis loop indicating the presence of mesoporous structure with slit-like pores. The surface areas of the samples were estimated as 7.7 m2/g, 23.2 m2/g , 31.2 m2/g , and 55.1 m2/g for the W, DCN, 10WDCN, and 10W1CDCN samples, respectively. Further, the corresponding relative pore volumes of the samples were observed as 0.006 cm3/g, 0.025 cm3/g, 0.040 cm3/g, and 0.075 cm3/g. Therefore, the results of the BET analysis further demonstrate that NCQDs were able to exfoliate DCN to produce few-layered thick DCN nanosheets as the surface area increased sharply from 23.2 m2/g to 55.1 m2/g after their addition. This enhanced surface area of the composites enhances their TC adsorption efficacy. Moreover, the enhanced surface area would provide more reaction sites and elevates the photocatalytic degradation of the composites.

The UV-DRS spectrum of the catalysts and the corresponding tauc plots is displayed in Fig. 6a and b. W exhibited an absorption edge at around 550 nm. The defect induction resulted in a bathochromic shift in the light absorption edge of the BCN sample, and an additional Urbach tail appeared for DCN. The shift in light absorption edge could be accredited to the modification in the electronic structure after defect induction [50]. After loading W on the surface of DCN, the light absorption edge was slightly right-shifted, which implies the heterostructure was successfully fabricated. But to our surprise, the visible light absorption edge of the 10W1CDCN sample is blue-shifted compared to the BCN and DCN. Moreover, from the tauc plot presented in Fig. 6b, the bandgaps of the samples were calculated. It was observed that the bandgap decreased from 2.85 eV (BCN) to 2.80 eV (DCN) after the defect induction. The W nanoplates possess a bandgap of 2.47 eV. The bandgap of 10WDCN slightly decreased to 2.79 eV after heterostructure formation. However, the bandgap of the 10W1CDCN sample was estimated as 2.88 eV. The increased bandgap could be ascribed to the quantum confinement effect induced due to the decreased layers [18]. Therefore, it is evident that the addition of NCQDs produced few-layered g-C3N4 nanosheets. Further, the UV absorption spectrum of NCQDs is displayed in Fig. 6c. The NCQDs present an absorption peak centred at around 350 nm due to the n-π* transition of the aromatic structures [58].

The photocatalytic efficiency of the as-prepared catalysts was investigated through the degradation of tetracycline antibiotics under the illumination of LED, and the findings have been summarized in Fig. 7
a. It could be noted that the samples were magnetically stirred in the darkness for 30 mins to ascertain the equilibrium before switching on the light. As is seen in Fig. 7a, sample W exhibited an almost negligible degradation efficiency of 15% after 60 min of LED illumination. The defect engineered material (DCN) could degrade 44% of TC, considerably higher than the efficiency of pristine BCN of 30%. However, the WDCN composites displayed an enhanced degradation efficiency compared to the pristine materials. The 5WDCN, 10WDCN, 20WDCN, and 30WDCN samples exhibited a degradation efficacy of 70%, 80%, 67%, and 70%, respectively, following 60 mins of light irradiation. It could be observed that the degradation performance initially increases with an increase in the W content till 10%, which is the optimum content of W. This enhancement in performance after composite formation can be accredited to the combined outcome of increased surface area and elevated spatial charge carrier separation. However, W loading beyond that point resulted in the reduction of degradation performance. This could have happened because of the light-shielding effect of the excessive WO3·nH2O nanoplates.

Further, the degradation kinetics of the process was explored through a series of experiments. The kinetic plot (as presented in Fig. 7b, between ln(C/C0) and time) could be efficiently fitted to the first-order degradation kinetics. Besides that, the apparent degradation rate constants for TC degradation for the catalysts were determined from the kinetic plot. It was observed that the 10WDCN exhibited a 15.2, 3.8, and 2.5-fold higher degradation efficacy as compared to those obtained in the presence of W, BCN, and DCN samples, respectively.

The practical application aspect of a photocatalyst warrants the catalyst dosage to be optimized for better outcomes. Therefore, experiments were carried out by varying the catalyst dosage in the range of 0.25–1.50 g/L, and the findings have been summarized in Fig. 7c. It was obvious that the efficacy of the catalyst sharply increased from 35% to 64% after 30 min light irradiation when the dosage was increased from 0.25 g/L to 1 g/L. But, further increase in dosage did not improve the performance of the catalyst. The initial increase in the catalyst dosage results in an elevated quantity of active reaction sites to accelerate the degradation process. Hence, the dose of 1 g/L was considered optimum and has been adopted for further studies.

The effect of the solution pH on the degradation performance was examined by adjusting the solution pH in the range of 2.9–10.8, and the findings are presented in Fig. 7d. The pH of the solution was adjusted with 0.1 N NaOH or 0.1 N HCl. It could be noted that the adsorption efficiency of the catalyst increased with an increase in the solution pH. However, at high pH values, the adsorption capacity drastically got reduced. Even though the adsorption efficiency reduces at high pH values, the degradation efficacy of the catalyst increases with the increasing pH values in general. It is well known that the TC molecule possesses three different functionalities with a distinct pKa value of 3.3, 7.7, and 9.7 [59,60]. Therefore, TC can be present in a cationic form in a pH less than 3.3, neutral (molecular form) in the pH range of 3.3–7.7, and in anionic form (HTC- and TC2-) at a pH greater than 7.7 [61]. Thus, it could be speculated that at a pH value greater than 9.7, both the catalyst and TC are present in a strong anionic form, and the electrostatic repulsion force prohibits the adsorption of TC. However, the increased hydroxide anion concentration at high pH values favors the oxidation of holes to produce hydroxyl radicals in the systems. This elevated concentration of hydroxyl radicals degrades the pollutant more efficiently at high pH values [59].

In addition, the effect of the presence of various anions during the degradation process was also examined through the addition of NaCl (0.5 mmol), Na2SO4 (3 mM), and Na2CO3 (3 mM) as chloride, sulfate, and carbonate source. As is seen in Fig. 8 a, the presence of carbonate anion had almost no impact on the photodegradation process, whereas chloride ions and sulfate ions reduced the degradation efficiency from 80% to 61% and 63%, respectively. The competitive adsorption between anions and TC on the catalyst surface may have contributed to the reduction in degradation efficiency [62].

For practical application, the performance evaluation of a photocatalyst under a real water matrix is of utmost importance. Therefore, degradation experiments were carried out in real water matrixes (sampling locations have been detailed in Fig. 8b and c) spiked with TC solution, and the observations are summarized in Fig. 8d. The characteristics of the real water samples have been summarized in Table s1. From Fig. 8d, it is evident that the TC degradation efficiency was enhanced in the presence of real water matrices. Particularly, the real wastewater displayed a superior degradation performance as compared to pond water and DI. This elevated performance could be ascribed to the elevated pH of the real water matrices (6.71 for pond water and 6.86 for real wastewater) and the presence of humic substances (HS) in higher concentrations. It has been observed that an increase in solution pH improves the degradation efficiency. Further, the presence of HS could enhance the degradation efficiency of TC due to the photosensitizing effect and its efficacy in accelerating ROS species production [63,64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NCQDs loading on photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the influence of NCQD loading on photocatalytic performance, composites were prepared by loading various amounts (namely 0.5, 1, and 1.5%) of NCQD on the already optimized 10WDCN sample. The photocatalytic activity of the as-synthesized composites was assessed through TC degradation, and the results are reported in Fig. 9 a. As seen in Fig. 9a, 0.5% NCQD loading did not alter the efficacy of the 10WDCN composite. Further increasing the loading to 1% improved the photocatalytic performance significantly. The 10W1CDCN sample could degrade 80% TC in just 30 min, as compared to 60 min for the 10WDCN sample. However, anymore additional NCQD loading had an adverse impact on the performance of the composite. Further, Fig. 9b shows that the degradation follows first-order kinetics, and the rate coefficients were determined from the kinetic plots. Fig. 9c compares the degradation rate constants of TC in the presence of different catalysts. It could be observed that the 10W1CDCN sample offered an excellent apparent rate constant of 0.044 min−1 which is 2.06, 5.2, 7.7, and 31.5-fold higher than the rate co-efficient of 10WDCN, DCN, BCN, and W, respectively. This enhancement in degradation efficiency after NCQD addition could be attributed to the dual role played by NCQD. NCQD could efficiently exfoliate the DCN nanosheets to produce few-layered DCN nanosheets. In addition, NCQD played the role of an electron mediator in the all-solid-state Z scheme heterostructure and accelerated the spatial charge carrier separation efficiency, which improved the performance.

In addition, the activity of the as-prepared photocatalyst in TC degradation was compared with a few recently published works, and the findings are reported in Table 1 . With an effort to normalize the efficiency of the catalyst, the turnover number (TON) and turnover frequency (TOF) values were also determined. In heterogeneous catalysis, TON could be defined as the moles of pollutant degraded per unit mass of the applied catalysis (moles/gcatalyst). Further, TOF is the moles of pollutant degraded per unit mass of the applied catalysis per unit time (moles/gcatalyst.min). Furthermore, to normalize the cost-effectiveness of the catalyst, a ratio was devised by dividing the TOF value by the wattage (TOF/W) of the light used in the study. From the calculated ratios, it could be said that the 10W1CDCN exhibited an excellent photocatalytic performance compared to the other catalysts.

To assess the reusability of the as-synthesized photocatalyst, a four-cycle recycling experiment was performed in TC degradation, and the result is presented in Fig. 10 a. After each successive cycle, the catalyst was separated through centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min, rinsed with DI multiple times and dried overnight at 60℃ in a drying oven. The dried material was further used in the next cycle. As is seen in Fig. 10a, the 10W1CDCN sample exhibited a satisfactory photocatalytic performance even after 4 cycles. The slight reduction in photocatalytic efficiency could be ascribed to the loss of material during the experimentation. Further, the XRD spectrum of the reused catalyst was collected to assess the stability of the composite. The XRD spectra reported in Fig. 10b shows that the catalyst retained its structure after a 4-cycle degradation experiment. Moreover, the comparison between the FTIR spectra (Fig. 10c) of the pristine and reused material revealed that the catalyst retained its structure after the recycling experiment. The TEM and FESEM images (Fig. 10d and e) also demonstrated the excellent stability of the reused material. Thus, it can be said that the catalyst possesses commendable reusability toward TC degradation.

The photocatalytic performance of a catalyst is strongly dependent on the charge carrier separation efficacy. Thus, photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were collected at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm, and the observations are reported in Fig. 11 a. As is seen, the BCN sample exhibited the highest PL intensity indicating the lowest electron-hole separation efficiency. However, W displayed the lowest PL intensity. Defect induction in the structure of BCN lowered the PL intensity significantly. The fabrication of the 10WDCN composite further reduced the PL intensity, which implies that the heterostructure fabrication could spatially separate the photo-generated charge carriers. Furthermore, after the addition of NCQD into the 10W1CDCN heterostructure, the PL intensity further decreased. This confirms that the NCQDs addition further accelerated the electron-hole separation efficiency.

In order to identify the active radicals accountable for the TC degradation, sequential radical scavenging tests were conducted employing EDTA-2Na, BQ, and IPA as h+, . O2
– , and . OH scavengers, respectively. As presented in Fig. 11b, it was observed that h+ and . O2
– was the main responsible radical on the process, as only 5 and 17% TC degradation was achieved after the addition of EDTA-2Na and BQ, respectively. Whereas, the . OH played a less significant role in the photocatalytic degradation process as the IPA addition slightly suppresses the degradation efficiency. Moreover, the presence of . OH was ascertained from the fluorescence intensity of the 2HTA produced from the interaction between TA and . OH. Fig. 11c shows the fluorescence spectra of the 2HTA generated after light illumination for pre-defined durations. The fluorescence intensity was observed to increase with time. Therefore, it is evident that . OH took part in the TC degradation. In addition, the generation of . O2
– was further confirmed from the EPR spectra (Fig. 11d) of the 10W1CDCN sample under darkness and light irradiation. No peak of . O2
– was observed under darkness, whereas strong peaks of . O2
– appeared upon visible light irradiation. Therefore, it could be said that . O2
– was produced when the 10W1CDCN sample was irradiated with visible light.

Further, EIS analysis of the as-synthesized photocatalysts were performed and the results have been reported in Fig. 12 . It is well known that the arc radius of the Nyquist plot represents the charge carrier separation efficiency of a catalyst i.e. the smaller the arc radius, the lower the charge transfer resistance [70]. As is seen in Fig. 12, the 10W1CDCN composite exhibited the smallest Nyquist arc radius as compared to the DCN and W sample, which indicates that the 10W1CDCN composite possesses the lowest photogenerated charge transfer resistance. Therefore, it can be said that the heterostructure fabrication successfully promoted the photogenerated charge carrier transmission.

The bandgap of DCN and W were estimated as 2.80 and 2.47 eV, respectively from the tauc plot (Fig. 6b). To elucidate the photocatalytic mechanism, the band edge of the materials were calculated according to the following formula [14]:
E
CB
= χ -

E

E
g
where χ is the absolute electronegativity of the semiconductor (6.91 eV for g-C3N4 and 6.59 eV for WO3), Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (4.5 eV), Eg is the bandgap of the semiconductor, ECB and EVB are the conduction and valence band position of the semiconductors, respectively. The CB edge of DCN and W was calculated as –1.01 and + 0.855 eV, whereas the VB positions were observed to lie at + 1.79 and + 3.325 eV, respectively. Based on the calculated band edge positions, a possible charge carrier mechanism of the 10W1CDCN sample is proposed (Fig. 13 ). Upon visible light irradiation, electrons from both the semiconductors jumped to the conduction band, leaving the holes behind. It should be noted that electrons in the CB of DCN exhibit enough potential to reduce oxygen to produce . O2 – radicals. On the other hand, holes present in W could oxidize H2O to produce . OH (potential more than 1.99 V). In the present scenario, if the composite followed a typical type II heterostructure, electrons of DCN would have accumulated in CB of W, whereas holes from the VB of W would have migrated to DCN. However, the migrated electron-holes do not possess enough potential to produce . O2 – and . OH. Therefore, it is imperative that the composite followed a Z scheme charge transfer pathway. In detail, the photo-generated electrons from the CB of W combined with the holes in the VB of DCN, and thus electron and holes with superior redox potential in DCN and W, respectively, are preserved. The addition of NCQDs could improve the charge separation efficiency of the composite in two ways: (i) the NCQDs could act as an electron mediator to form an all-solid-state Z scheme heterostructure, (ii) simultaneously, NCQDs could act as an electron sink, and therefore the photogenerated electrons in the CB of DCN would migrate to the NCQDs resulting in further spatial separation of the electrons. The 10W1CDCN composite may have demonstrated excellent charge carrier separation due to the synergistic effect of the phenomenon mentioned above.

In summary, out of the various composites prepared, 10W1CDCN exhibited the best TC degradation performance under LED light illumination. The 10W1CDCN sample could degrade 80% of the initial TC concentration in 30 min and had a degradation rate coefficient of 0.044 min−1. This enhanced degradation efficiency could be ascribed to the synergistic effect of increased surface area and superior charge carrier separation efficacy of the Z scheme. The introduction of NCQDs in the composite further enhanced the charge separation where NCQDs acted as an electron mediator. Further, the TC degradation efficiency of the 10WDCN sample in real water matrices (pond water and real wastewater) was also examined. The degradation efficiency was significantly enhanced in the presence of real water matrices. This improvement in efficiency could be attributed to the higher pH value of real water samples and the presence of humic substances. In addition, the composite displayed excellent stability even after a four-cycle TC degradation. The radical scavenging experiments revealed that h+ and . O2 – were the main involved reactive species, whereas . OH played a less significant role in the degradation process. At last, based on the results of the radical trapping experiment, a plausible all-solid-state Z scheme charge transfer pathway has been proposed. This study would encourage to further explore this novel trait of NCQDs.
